name: Francis Wayland Thurston
facts: The account was found among his papers; he is called "the late" Francis Wayland Thurston.
He was of Boston.
George Gammell Angell, who died in the winter of 1926-27, was his grand-uncle.
His knowledge of the matter began in the winter of 1926-27 with Professor Angell's death.
He was his grand-uncle's heir and executor.
He moved Professor Angell's entire set of files and boxes to his quarters in Boston.
Among the papers he found a puzzling locked box, whose key was on the personal ring the professor always carried in his pocket.
The box contained a queer clay bas-relief and disjointed jottings, ramblings, and cuttings.
He resolved to search out the eccentric sculptor responsible for the bas-relief.
His single glimpse of forbidden eons flashed out from an accidental piecing together of an old newspaper item and the notes of a dead professor.
He hoped no one else would accomplish this piecing together, and vowed never to knowingly supply a link in so hideous a chain.
fate: Deceased — the account is presented as found among the papers of "the late" Francis Wayland Thurston.

sources.quote: Found among the papers of the late Francis Wayland Thurston, of Boston.
That glimpse, like all dread glimpses of truth, flashed out from an accidental piecing together of separated things—in this case an old newspaper item and the notes of a dead professor.
I hope that no one else will accomplish this piecing out; certainly, if I live, I shall never knowingly supply a link in so hideous a chain.

My knowledge of the thing began in the winter of 1926-27 with the death of my grand-uncle, George Gammell Angell, Professor Emeritus of Semitic languages in Brown University, Providence, Rhode Island.

As my granduncle's heir and executor, for he died a childless widower, I was expected to go over his papers with some thoroughness; and for that purpose moved his entire set of files and boxes to my quarters in Boston.
It had been locked, and I did not find the key till it occurred to me to examine the personal ring which the professor carried always in his pocket.
For what could be the meaning of the queer clay bas-relief and the disjointed jottings, ramblings, and cuttings which I found?
I resolved to search out the eccentric sculptor responsible for this apparent disturbance of an old man's peace of mind.

name: Boston
facts: Francis Wayland Thurston, among whose papers the account was found, was of Boston.
The narrator's quarters, to which he moved Professor Angell's files and boxes, were in Boston.

sources.quote: Found among the papers of the late Francis Wayland Thurston, of Boston.

As my granduncle's heir and executor, for he died a childless widower, I was expected to go over his papers with some thoroughness; and for that purpose moved his entire set of files and boxes to my quarters in Boston.

name: George Gammell Angell
facts: He was the narrator's grand-uncle.
He was Professor Emeritus of Semitic languages in Brown University, Providence, Rhode Island.
He died in the winter of 1926-27.
He was widely known as an authority on ancient inscriptions and was frequently resorted to by the heads of prominent museums.
He died at the age of ninety-two, and local interest was intensified by the obscurity of the cause of death.
He was stricken whilst returning from the Newport boat, falling suddenly after having been jostled by a nautical-looking negro.
Physicians found no visible disorder and concluded that an obscure lesion of the heart, induced by the brisk ascent of a steep hill, was responsible for the end.
His home was in Williams Street, reached by a short cut from the waterfront through queer dark courts on a precipitous hillside.
He died a childless widower.
The narrator believed the professor intended to keep silent regarding what he knew and would have destroyed his notes had sudden death not seized him.
The writing accompanying the bas-relief was in his most recent hand and made no pretense to literary style.
His main document was headed "CTHULHU CULT" in painstakingly printed characters.
His manuscript was divided into two sections: the 1925 dream and dream work of H. A. Wilcox, and the narrative of Inspector John R. Legrasse with notes on it and Prof. Webb's account.
On March 1st, 1925, a thin, dark young man — Henry Anthony Wilcox — called upon him bearing the singular clay bas-relief, then exceedingly damp and fresh.
He recognized Wilcox as the youngest son of an excellent family slightly known to him.
Wilcox's rejoinder impressed him enough to make him recall and record it verbatim.
fate: Died in the winter of 1926-27, aged ninety-two, stricken after being jostled by a nautical-looking negro; physicians blamed an obscure lesion of the heart, and the cause of death remained obscure.

sources.quote: My knowledge of the thing began in the winter of 1926-27 with the death of my grand-uncle, George Gammell Angell, Professor Emeritus of Semitic languages in Brown University, Providence, Rhode Island.

Professor Angell was widely known as an authority on ancient inscriptions, and had frequently been resorted to by the heads of prominent museums; so that his passing at the age of ninety-two may be recalled by many.
Locally, interest was intensified by the obscurity of the cause of death.

The professor had been stricken whilst returning from the Newport boat; falling suddenly, as witnesses said, after having been jostled by a nautical-looking negro who had come from one of the queer dark courts on the precipitous hillside which formed a short cut from the waterfront to the deceased's home in Williams Street.

Physicians were unable to find any visible disorder, but concluded after perplexed debate that some obscure lesion of the heart, induced by the brisk ascent of so steep a hill by so elderly a man, was responsible for the end.
As my granduncle's heir and executor, for he died a childless widower, I was expected to go over his papers with some thoroughness
I think that the professor, too, intended to keep silent regarding the part he knew, and that he would have destroyed his notes had not sudden death seized him.
The writing accompanying this oddity was, aside from a stack of press cuttings, in Professor Angell's most recent hand; and made no pretense to literary style.
What seemed to be the main document was headed "CTHULHU CULT" in characters painstakingly printed to avoid the erroneous reading of a word so unheard-of.

This manuscript was divided into two sections, the first of which was headed "1925—Dream and Dream Work of H. A. Wilcox, 7 Thomas St., Providence, R. I.," and the second, "Narrative of Inspector John R. Legrasse, 121 Bienville St., New Orleans, La., at 1908 A. A. S. Mtg—Notes on Same, & Prof. Webb's Acct."

It appears that on March 1st, 1925, a thin, dark young man of neurotic and excited aspect had called upon Professor Angell bearing the singular clay bas-relief, which was then exceedingly damp and fresh.
His card bore the name of Henry Anthony Wilcox, and my uncle had recognized him as the youngest son of an excellent family slightly known to him

Young Wilcox's rejoinder, which impressed my uncle enough to make him recall and record it verbatim, was of a fantastically poetic cast which must have typified his whole conversation, and which I have since found highly characteristic of him.

name: monster
facts: A figure of evidently pictorial intent was depicted above apparent hieroglyphics on the clay bas-relief.
The figure seemed to be a sort of monster, or a symbol representing a monster, of a form which only a diseased fancy could conceive.
To the narrator's imagination it yielded simultaneous pictures of an octopus, a dragon, and a human caricature.
A pulpy, tentacled head surmounted a grotesque and scaly body with rudimentary wings.
The general outline of the whole made it most shockingly frightful.
Behind the figure was a vague suggestion of a Cyclopean architectural background.

sources.quote: Above these apparent hieroglyphics was a figure of evidently pictorial intent, though its impressionistic execution forbade a very clear idea of its nature.
It seemed to be a sort of monster, or symbol representing a monster, of a form which only a diseased fancy could conceive.
If I say that my somewhat extravagant imagination yielded simultaneous pictures of an octopus, a dragon, and a human caricature, I shall not be unfaithful to the spirit of the thing.
A pulpy, tentacled head surmounted a grotesque and scaly body with rudimentary wings; but it was the general outline of the whole which made it most shockingly frightful.
Behind the figure was a vague suggestion of a Cyclopean architectural background.

name: Henry Anthony Wilcox
facts: The first section of Professor Angell's manuscript was headed "1925—Dream and Dream Work of H. A. Wilcox, 7 Thomas St., Providence, R. I."
On March 1st, 1925, he called upon Professor Angell bearing the singular clay bas-relief, which was then exceedingly damp and fresh.
He was a thin, dark young man of neurotic and excited aspect.
His card bore the name of Henry Anthony Wilcox.
He was the youngest son of an excellent family slightly known to Professor Angell.
He had latterly been studying sculpture at the Rhode Island School of Design.
He lived alone at the Fleur-de-Lys Building near the Rhode Island School of Design.
He was a precocious youth of known genius but great eccentricity, and from childhood excited attention through the strange stories and odd dreams he related.
He called himself "psychically hypersensitive"; the staid folk of the ancient commercial city dismissed him as merely "queer".
Never mingling much with his kind, he had dropped gradually from social visibility and was known only to a small group of esthetes from other towns.
Even the Providence Art Club, anxious to preserve its conservatism, had found him quite hopeless.
On his visit he abruptly asked Professor Angell to identify the hieroglyphics on the bas-relief, speaking in a dreamy, stilted manner.
He said he made the bas-relief the night before in a dream of strange cities, and that dreams are older than brooding Tyre, the contemplative Sphinx, or garden-girdled Babylon.

sources.quote: the first of which was headed "1925—Dream and Dream Work of H. A. Wilcox, 7 Thomas St., Providence, R. I.,"

It appears that on March 1st, 1925, a thin, dark young man of neurotic and excited aspect had called upon Professor Angell bearing the singular clay bas-relief, which was then exceedingly damp and fresh.

His card bore the name of Henry Anthony Wilcox, and my uncle had recognized him as the youngest son of an excellent family slightly known to him, who had latterly been studying sculpture at the Rhode Island School of Design and living alone at the Fleur-de-Lys Building near that institution.
Wilcox was a precocious youth of known genius but great eccentricity, and had from childhood excited attention through the strange stories and odd dreams he was in the habit of relating.
He called himself "psychically hypersensitive", but the staid folk of the ancient commercial city dismissed him as merely "queer".
Never mingling much with his kind, he had dropped gradually from social visibility, and was now known only to a small group of esthetes from other towns.
Even the Providence Art Club, anxious to preserve its conservatism, had found him quite hopeless.
On the occasion of the visit, ran the professor's manuscript, the sculptor abruptly asked for the benefit of his host's archeological knowledge in identifying the hieroglyphics on the bas-relief.
He said, "It is new, indeed, for I made it last night in a dream of strange cities; and dreams are older than brooding Tyre, or the contemplative Sphinx, or garden-girdled Babylon."
